item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When you mentioned this:

The structure of SCP-3065 is humanoid and shifts in real time to match the shape

I immediately thought of Junji Ito's Amigara Fault manga.

In my opinion this skip recreates that kind of horrifying feeling quite nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only at the Foundation could you say "cave that eats people" in a dismissive fashion. Though, they weren't technically correct - it binds itself with its victim's physiology as well. That makes me wonder if it could become smarter if it also manages to inherit the mind…

I quite liked this, but wonder why the hiker was the first victim. A hiking trail would have had people there in the past, so this would have to be a relatively new phenomenon. An older phenomenon might increase the horror of being transported there.

I like this one, it's inventive, and doesn't go on too long. I appreciate not getting bogged down in the details of how somebody might conceivably contrive some way of escape, both because this would affect how simple and readable the article is, and because leaving it vague and potentially inescapable adds to its potential threat.

I also like that the researchers decline to send more people into it to investigate. It IS wasteful to feed people to the cave and then experiment with ways of getting them out.

item.title: Re: Hm
item.content:encoded: It's a clear reference to the term 'man cave', a place a man would supposedly go to retire for leisure time away from family or whatever. Of course, in this case, the man is both trapped in the cave, and it's man-shaped, so it works both ways.

item.title: Re: Hm
item.content:encoded: I get it. It's a cave shaped like a man.

item.title: Hm
item.content:encoded: After reading the final words said by the foundation personnel, maybe you should change the name of the article to “cannibalistic cavern” or something. Man cave doesn’t really have to do anything with the article other than the cave part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the premises can be difficult to comprehend at first reading, it ultimately got its message down.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: darth dxVxb, as per the site rules and required reading, spam and contentless posts are against the rules. Please refrain from making such posts in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I considered an "escape" story in a later draft, but when I started writing it got bogged down with details and I eventually scrapped it. Admittedly, I took the easy way out by killing Collins and cutting off the test logs.

Part of the problem is that I wasn't sure what should happen if the person escapes. Does the cave continue to mimic them? Because then you basically have a permanent (though tiny) earthquake. Or it could just deactivate - this is a little boring but a satisfying escape log would make up for it.

I'm writing something else right now, but I'll probably come back to this in a few weeks and rewrite the ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing is technically solid, but I feel like this wastes a potentially interesting premise. I really thought the test logs would produce some great material around trying to get people out without them dying (a bit like the reverse voodoo doll) but they concentrated on the least interesting part of the anomaly, and then cut off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture is by Todd VerBeek, licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 4.0 International and found on Wikimedia Commons. (It's actually in Michigan, but I'd already written the details about BC.)

Originally this was pretty short with no logs, but Moklin advised I make it more of a story than a description. Overall I like how it turned out, although there are a few bits I'm not 100% satisfied with. (Specifically, if you drill down to the cave and then the person moves a bit, the shaft may not still be connected. And if it is, it'll be piercing a different part of their body…) Like a lot of anomalies, the mechanics start getting a little fuzzy if you inspect it from every angle. I ended up going with "less is more", and it's good enough for jazz.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked the log and the ending note's implications for the rest of the story made it sufficiently interesting for me to upvote. It's put together very well technically and I didn't mind the premature reaction by the regional authority to dubbing it contained and moving on - that's probably the most interesting bit of this. As a sample of body horror it's not too gratuitous and also pretty original in that it's rocks that mush and consume the fleshlings, which is a pretty cool concept that's realized well with the victim in the incident report.

+1 nice and solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not the most in depth narrative ever but the idea is novel and the execution well done. You need to post the source for your image btw.
